mission.title: Install and Configure Node Exporter (Prometheus) on Kubernetes
mission.description: Node Exporter is a Prometheus exporter that collects and exposes hardware and OS metrics from *NIX kernels. Installing Node Exporter allows you to monitor your machine's performance and resource usage effectively.

steps.title: Add the Prometheus Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Prometheus Helm repository to your Helm configuration to access the Node Exporter chart.
```bash
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Node Exporter using Helm
steps.description:
Install Node Exporter in a dedicated namespace called 'monitoring'. This command will deploy Node Exporter with the specified version.
```bash
helm install node-exporter prometheus-community/prometheus-node-exporter --version 1.10.2 --namespace monitoring --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Node Exporter pod is running properly in the monitoring namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace monitoring
```

steps.title: Expose Node Exporter for external access
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access Node Exporter metrics from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/node-exporter 9100:9100 --namespace monitoring
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the Node Exporter pod running in the 'monitoring' namespace, and you will be able to access the metrics at http://localhost:9100/metrics. Additionally, resource limits will be configured as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment node-exporter --namespace monitoring
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: 100m
#     memory: 128Mi
#   requests:
#     cpu: 50m
#     memory: 64Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Node Exporter Helm release from the monitoring namespace to remove the deployment and associated resources.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Node Exporter.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the monitoring namespace and verify that all resources have been cleaned up.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Node Exporter deployment.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the latest version of Node Exporter.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Node Exporter pod is running the new version properly in the monitoring namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when the Node Exporter pod fails to start repeatedly. Check the pod logs for errors using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace monitoring
```

troubleshooting.title: Metrics not being collected
troubleshooting.description:
If metrics are not being collected, verify that the Node Exporter service is running and accessible. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get services --namespace monitoring
```

troubleshooting.title: Port forwarding not working
troubleshooting.description: If you cannot access Node Exporter metrics via port forwarding, ensure that the port forwarding command is still running and check for any firewall rules that may be blocking access.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the Node Exporter pod is being terminated due to Out Of Memory (OOM) errors, consider adjusting the resource limits set in the deployment. Check the pod status with:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace monitoring
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
